title: Chemical Replicating Systems and Artificial Life

lines.text: Section eight. Chemical replicating systems — life-like systems built from chemistry.
If life started with self-replicating molecules and built up cellular complexity from there, can we recapitulate that ascent in the laboratory?
The work on chemical replicating systems takes the question seriously.
There are two main strands.
Strand one: building cell-like compartments out of pure chemistry.

Lipid molecules — small amphiphilic molecules with a hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tails — spontaneously self-assemble in water into bilayer vesicles, miniature spherical compartments whose interior is separated from the exterior by a lipid membrane just like a real cell.
Researchers have shown that synthetic lipid vesicles can grow by accumulating additional lipid molecules from their environment, and that they can divide spontaneously under gentle shear forces.
Growth and division — two of the hallmarks of cellular life — emerging from pure chemistry, with no biology involved.
This is a striking demonstration that the cellular form is not biologically special; it is what amphiphilic molecules do in water.
Strand two: putting replicating chemistry inside those compartments.

A natural target for a self-templating molecule is a ribozyme capable of polymerising RNA — an RNA enzyme that takes a single-stranded RNA template and copies it into a complementary strand using free nucleotides from the surrounding medium.
If such a ribozyme can copy any RNA, it can in principle copy itself, achieving self-replication entirely without proteins or DNA.
Engineering ribozymes with this kind of broad RNA-replicating activity has been a research target for years; partial successes exist, full self-replication remains the holy grail.
The textbook also describes a particularly elegant experiment that touches the boundary between artificial chemistry and biology.
Researchers built lipid vesicles enclosing a chemical metabolism — specifically the formose reaction, which takes formaldehyde and produces complex carbohydrates.
The carbohydrates the vesicle produced could diffuse out and trigger the quorum-sensing system of the bacterium Vibrio harveyi, causing the bacterium to bioluminesce.
So a non-living chemical "cell" — a lipid vesicle full of synthetic chemistry — successfully communicated with a living bacterium and made it light up.
This is a tiny but real demonstration of artificial life-like systems interacting with biology, and the authors call out this kind of work as an important emerging area.
What is the synthetic biology relevance of all of this?
Two things, I think.
First, it sharpens our sense of what is essential to life and what is incidental.
If lipid vesicles grow and divide on their own, then compartment formation is not a biological mystery; it is chemistry.
If ribozymes can be engineered to replicate, then we will eventually be able to draw the boundary between non-life and life much more precisely than we can today.
Second, it opens up genuinely new chassis for synthetic biology.
An engineered chemical cell — never alive, but performing the functions of metabolism, replication, and signalling — is a different kind of platform from a Mycoplasma with a synthetic genome.
It is fully reproducible from first principles, fully reset-able, with no evolutionary history of its own.
Whether such platforms will ever be more useful than engineered natural cells for practical applications remains to be seen.

But as a research programme — and as a way of testing what we actually understand about the chemical basis of life — chemical replicating systems are among the most fascinating threads in modern synthetic biology.
End of chapter four.
